---
title: "Lecture 5: Deep learning"
subtitle: >
  {{< var course.code >}}, {{< var course.term >}}
number-sections: false
---

## Lecture overview
### Learning outcomes
- TODO

### Pre-class work
::: {.content-hidden when-profile="student" when-profile="ta"}
#### Instructors
1. TODO

#### Coordinators
1. TODO
:::

::: {.content-hidden when-profile="student"}
#### TAs
1. TODO
:::

#### Learners
1. TODO

### Lecture timeline
::: {.list-table}
- - Topic
  - Time
  - Materials

- - Admin
  - 5 min

- - [Differences from previous lectures](#sec-prev-lectures)
  - 5 min

- - [Activity: Google Teachable Machine](#sec-teachable-machine)
  - 10 min
  - [Website: Google Teachable Machine](https://teachablemachine.withgoogle.com/train/image)

- - [Biological and artificial neural networks](#sec-neural)
  - 5 min

- - [Weights](#sec-weights)
  - 5 min

- - [Activity: Neural networks](#sec-neural-activity)
  - 10 min

- - [Scale of neural networks](#sec-scale)
  - 5 min

- - [Bias](#sec-bias)
  - 20 min
  
- - [Wrap-up](#sec-wrap-up)
  - 10 min

- - Admin
  - 5 min
:::

### Post-class work
::: {.content-hidden when-profile="student" when-profile="ta"}
#### Instructors
1. TODO

#### Coordinators
1. TODO
:::

::: {.content-hidden when-profile="student"}
#### TAs
1. TODO
:::

#### Learners
1. TODO

## Differences from previous lectures{#sec-prev-lectures}
- In machine learning, we start by choosing which features we want to use to train our model.
- However, identifying and engineering features manually can be very time consuming, and may require expert knowledge.

:::{.callout-tip}
### Activity: Choose the features
What kinds of features would you use to differentiate between images of cats and dogs?

::: {#fig-cat-dog layout-ncol=2}
![](../images/lecture-05_cat.png)

![](../images/lecture-05_dog.jpg)

Images of a cat (Ripley) and a dog (Midnight).
:::
:::

- It can be very difficult to extract useful features from our data!
- Instead of trying to come up with the features ourselves, it might be better to give the raw image to the model, e.g. by making each individual pixel a feature, and let it figure out the features as part of the training process.

## Activity: Google Teachable Machine{#sec-teachable-machine}
- TODO what are we trying to get across here? is this just another example of deep learning?
- TODO formalize instructions
- [TODO: any issues with using the webcam/images of prof or students? alternatively have a set of images ready to upload]
- have prof or volunteer student come up to front and use webcam to record images of them doing 2 distinct things (smiling/frowning, left/right of screen, wearing hat/not wearing hat, etc). train model, then test whether it can differentiate the 2 distinct things
- with ~100 images per class and 2 classes, takes ~2 min to train. do not switch tabs during training as it will stop
- after demonstration at front, could let students try on their own:
  - add more classes
  - train with object added to/removed from background between two different unrelated classes (e.g. smiling with water bottle, frowning without) and see how adding/removing the object during testing changes the accuracy
  - train with one person smiling/frowning and use another person smiling/frowning to test
    - see if model improves if both people are used to train
  - do something that wasn't in either class and see which class it is more like (e.g. shocked face). why do you think that is/what features probably make that the case? (e.g. if shocked is most like smiling, eyebrow movements might be similar, etc...)

## Biological and artificial neural networks{#sec-neural}
- [In the image above](#fig-cat-dog), you were probably able to identify which picture was a cat and which picture was a dog pretty quickly. Humans are able to do these kinds of classification tasks very efficiently.
- To simulate the same level of performance with a machine, we took inspiration from biological neural networks (our brains!) when designing a new type of model: **artificial neural networks**.

::: {#fig-neurons layout-ncol=2}
![](../images/lecture-05_bio-neuron.png)

![](../images/lecture-05_art-neuron.png)

Biological and artificial neurons. Base image of biological neuron from OpenClipart-Vectors on Pixabay.
:::

- Neural networks are made up of neurons. Just like the neurons that make up our brains, each neuron takes some number of **inputs** and produces an **output**. Inputs can be features or outputs from other neurons. In the image above, the artificial neuron has three inputs ($x_1, x_2, x_3$), and its output is $y$.

::: {#fig-network}
![](../images/lecture-05_network.png)

A neural network.
:::

- We can stack layers of neurons.
  - We input all of the features into the network in the first layer (the **input layer**).
  - The last layer is called the **output layer**. Each neuron in this layer outputs something we want to know, like a prediction of whether an image is a cat or a dog.
  - We can add layers of neurons between the input and output layers: these are called **hidden layers**. The more hidden layers we add, the better the neural network gets at modelling complex relationships between features and labels.
  - We call a neural network with lots of layers a **deep** neural network - this is why we call the overall approach **deep learning**.

## Weights{#sec-weights}
- Inside each neuron, inputs are **added together** to produce an output.

:::{.callout-tip}
### Activity: Think, pair, share
TODO Consider the last neuron Are all inputs equally important?
:::

- Not every input is equally as important to a neuron. For example, it might be more valuable to **weigh** inputs differently TODO $w_1, w_2, w_3$

- Weights are a neural network's **parameters**: during the training process, the network learns how important each input is and thus what weight it should have.

- TODO any discussion on bias term? imo it's just some added complexity that doesn't really add to the understanding - weights are the big thing here.


- TODO how are parameters learned? feedforward/backprop
  - animated visualization?
- TODO SGD??

## Activity: Neural networks{#sec-neural-activity}
- TODO: some interactive example of a neural network where students are trying to produce a specific result
- TODO effect of adding/removing neurons
- TODO effect of adding/removing layers

## Scale of neural networks{#sec-scale}
- TODO if all pixels are features, this is a lot of features
- TODO fitting models this large is challenging, requires a lot of data (to get the huge number of parameters right) and compute (to finish training in a reasonable amount of time)
- TODO introduce idea of compute and history of compute
- TODO advancements in neural networks
- TODO some sense that we don't actually understand what's in all the neurons

## Bias{#sec-bias}
- TODO what is bias? intro to bias
- TODO bias in training data causing biased model, eg face detection
- TODO some interactive thing where students can see the effect of biased training data

- TODO anything else?
  - CNN demo suggested but wouldn't take long enough
  - SGD suggested

## Wrap-up{#sec-wrap-up}

:::{.callout-tip}
### Activity: Discussion
- TODO
:::
